title: Canada — PIPEDA / Bill C-27 Consumer Privacy Protection Act (CPPA)
official_title: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (S.C. 2000, c. 5) and proposed Consumer Privacy Protection Act (Bill C-27, Part 1)
status: PIPEDA in force; CPPA (C-27) in legislative process
overview: Canada's current federal private-sector privacy law is PIPEDA (2000). In 2022, the government introduced Bill C-27 which would replace PIPEDA with the Consumer Privacy Protection Act (CPPA), introduce the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA), and create a new Personal Information and Data Protection Tribunal. As of early 2026, C-27 has not received Royal Assent and PIPEDA remains the operative federal law. Quebec, British Columbia, and Alberta have substantially similar provincial laws that apply within their provinces.

principles: Accountability — designate responsible individual, implement privacy policies
Identifying purposes — identify purposes before or at time of collection
Consent — obtain knowledge and consent of individual (with exceptions)
Limiting collection — collect only what is necessary
Limiting use, disclosure, and retention — use only for identified purposes
Accuracy — keep personal information accurate, complete, up-to-date
Safeguards — protect by security safeguards appropriate to sensitivity
Openness — make policies and practices readily available
Individual access — right to access and challenge accuracy of personal information
Challenging compliance — individual can address non-compliance to the designated person
pipeda_overview.consent_model: Knowledge and consent required. Implied consent can be used for less sensitive information or where purpose is obvious. Express consent required for sensitive information. Withdrawal of consent must be allowed.
pipeda_overview.breach_notification: Mandatory breach reporting to OPC and individuals where real risk of significant harm (since 2018).
pipeda_overview.enforcement: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC). OPC can investigate complaints and issue findings but cannot impose fines — must go to Federal Court for binding orders.

rights: Enhanced right of access — electronic format, explain automated decisions
Right to erasure/de-indexing (new and broader than PIPEDA)
Right to withdraw consent with immediate effect
consent_and_transparency: Consent cannot be bundled with terms of service
Business contact information exemption maintained but narrowed
Exceptions for de-identified data without consent
obligations: Privacy management program required (similar to GDPR accountability)
Privacy impact assessments for high-impact processing
Algorithmic transparency — explain automated decision systems on request

enforcement.opc_powers: Enhanced OPC investigative and compliance order powers
enforcement.tribunal: New Personal Information and Data Protection Tribunal for appeals and enforcement
enforcement.fines: Up to 5% of global revenue or $25M (whichever greater) for most serious violations; up to 3% or $10M for other violations
enforcement.private_right_of_action: Individuals can bring claims after OPC finding

provincial_laws.quebec_law_25: Loi modernisant des dispositions législatives en matière de protection des renseignements personnels (Law 25) — fully in force since September 2023. Most GDPR-like of Canadian laws: mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments, DPO equivalent, explicit consent requirements, right to portability and de-indexation, high penalties ($25M or 4% of worldwide turnover).
provincial_laws.alberta: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) — substantially similar to PIPEDA. Enforced by Alberta Information and Privacy Commissioner.
provincial_laws.bc: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA BC) — substantially similar to PIPEDA. Enforced by BC Information and Privacy Commissioner.

aida_status: Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (Bill C-27, Part 3) — proposed alongside CPPA. Would regulate high-impact AI systems. As of early 2026, Bill C-27 has not passed. See canada_aida baseline for AI-specific provisions.

penalty_structure.pipeda_current: No fines — OPC cannot impose penalties. Federal Court can issue orders. Summary conviction offence: up to $100,000 per offence.
penalty_structure.cppa_proposed: Up to 5% of global revenue or C$25M for most serious violations. Up to 3% or C$10M for other violations.
penalty_structure.quebec_law_25: Up to C$25M or 4% of worldwide turnover.

cross_references.relevance: Canada has EU adequacy status under GDPR. CPPA is designed to maintain this adequacy. Quebec Law 25 is closest in approach to GDPR.
AIDA and CPPA are in the same Bill C-27. High-impact AI systems processing personal information must comply with both CPPA/PIPEDA and AIDA.
Both protect consumers' data from commercial use. PIPEDA/CPPA use an implied consent model for less sensitive data; CCPA uses opt-out model. Different rights and obligations.
